title: 第 97 節
en: 16. 'It is enough, let him know this to be true.
17. 'Let him who knows this not communicate, nor dine, nor amuse himself with any one who does not know it.'
Then follow some more rules as to the reading of the Veda in general :

18. 'When the old water that stood round the roots of trees is dried up (after about the month of Pausha, January to February2) he should not read ; nor (at any time) in the morning or in the afternoon, when the shadows meet (he should begin at sunrise so soon as the shadows divide, and end in the evening before they fall together). Nor should he read 3 when a cloud has risen ; and when there is an unseasonable rain (after the months of Sva.va.na. and Bhadrapada, August and September 4) he should stop his Vedic reading for three nights. Nor should he at that time tell stories, not even during the night, nor should he glory in his knowledge.
19. ' This (the Veda thus learnt and studied) is the name of that Great Being ; and he who thus knows the name of that Great Being, he becomes Brahman, yea, he becomes Brahman.'
1 Apastamba-sutras, translated by Biihler, p. 92 (I, 2, 30, 4).
2 Apastamba-sutras, translated by Biihler, p. 33 (I, 3, 9, 2).
3 Apastamba-sutras, translated by Biihler, p. 44 (I, 3, n, 31).
4 Apastamba-sutras, translated by Biihler, p. 33 (I, 3, 9, 1).
kaushItaki-brahmaaaupanishad.
First Adhyaya.

i. A'itra Gangyayani1, forsooth, wishing to perform a sacrifice, chose Arum (Uddalaka2, to be his chief priest). But Arum sent his son, 6Vetaketu, and said : ' Perform the sacrifice for him.' When 6Vetaketu 3 had arrived, A"itra asked him : ' Son of Gautama4, is there a hidden place in the world where you are able to place me, or is it the other way, and are you going to place me in the world to which it (that other way) leads5?'

1 It is difficult to determine whether iTitra's name was Gahgyayani or Gargyaya/zi. Professor Weber adopted first Gargyaya/zi (Indische Studien I, p. 395), afterwards Gahgyayani (ibid. II, 395). Professor Cowell adopts Gahgyayani, but he tells us that the Telugu MS. reads Gargyaya/zi throughout, and the other MSS. B, C do so occasionally. The commentator explains Gahgyayani as the descendant (yuvapatyam) of Gahgya. I confess a preference for Gargyaya/zi, because both Gahga and Gahgya are names of rare occurrence in ancient Vedic literature, but I admit that for that very reason the transition of Gahgyayani into Gargyayam is perhaps more intelligible than that of Gargyaya/zi into Gahgyayani.
2 Cf. Kh. Up. V, n, 2 ; B/-zh. Ar. VI, 2, 1.
3 Cf. Kh. Up. V, 3 ; VI, 1. 4 B/-zh. Ar. VI, 2, 4.

5 The question put by A'itra to -SVetaketu is very obscure, and was probably from the first intended to be obscure in its very wording. What Altra wished to ask we can gather from other passages in the Upanishads, where we see another royal sage, Pravaha/za Gaivali {Kh. Up. V, 3 ; Brz'h. Ar. VI, 2), enlightening -Svetaketu on the future life. That future life is reached by two roads ;
272 KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD He answered and said : 'I do not know this. But, let me ask the master.' Having approached his father, he asked: 'Thus has A"itra asked me; how shall I answer ? '

one, the Devapatha, leading to the world of Brahman (the conditioned), beyond which there lies one other stage only, represented by knowledge of and identity with the unconditioned Brahman; the other leading to the world of the fathers, and from thence, after the reward of good works has been consumed, back to a new round of mundane existence. There is a third road for creatures which live and die, worms, insects, and creeping things, but they are of little consequence. Now it is quite clear that the knowledge which king iTitra possesses, and which -Svetaketu does not possess, is that of the two roads after death, sometimes called the right and the left, or the southern and northern roads. These roads are fully described in the A^andogya-upanishad and in the Bn'had-ararayaka, with certain variations, yet on the whole with the same purpose. The northern or left road, called also the path of the Devas, passes on from light and day to the bright half of the moon; the southern or right road, called also the path of the fathers, passes on from smoke and night to the dark half of the moon. Both roads therefore meet in the moon, but diverge afterwards. While the northern road passes by the six months when the sun moves towards the north, through the sun, (moon,) and the lightning to the world of Brahman, the southern passes by the six months when the sun moves towards the south, to the world of the fathers, the ether, and the moon. The great difference, however, between the two roads is, that while those who travel on the former do not return again to a new life on earth, but reach in the end a true knowledge of the unconditioned Brahman, those who pass on to the world of the fathers and the moon return to earth to be born again and again.

The question therefore which A^itra addresses to Svetaketu can refer to these two roads only, and though the text is very corrupt, and was so evidently even at the time when the commentary was written, we must try to restore it in accordance with the teaching imparted by A^itra in what follows. I propose to read : Gautamasya putra, asti sa/ravn'taw loke yasmin ma dhasyasy anyatamo vadhva tasya (or yasya) ma loke dhasyasi, ' Is there a hidden place in the world where you (by your sacrificing and teaching) are able to I ADHYAYA, 2. 27^ A Aru;/i said : ' I also do not know this. Only after having learnt the proper portion of the Veda in Antra's own dwelling, shall we obtain what others give us (knowledge). Come, we will both go.'

Having said this he took fuel in his hand (like a pupil), and approached TTitra Gangyayani, saying : 'May I come near to you?' He replied: ' You are worthy of Brahman \ O Gautama, because you were not led away by pride. Come hither, I shall make you know clearly.'

2. And iTitra said : All who depart from this world (or this body) go to the moon 2. In the former, (the bright) half, the moon delights in their spirits ; in the other, (the dark) half, the moon sends them on place me, or is it the other way, and will you place me in the world to which it leads?' Even thus the text is by no means satisfactory, but it is better than anyam aho vadhva, adopted by the commentator and explained by him : Is there a hidden place in that world in which you will place me as another, i. e. as different from the whole world or identical with the whole world, and, if as different, then having bound me (vadhva =baddhva) and made me a different person ? We may read anyataro for anyatamo vadhva. The commentator sums up the question as referring to a hidden or not hidden place, where -STitra should be placed as another person or not another person, as bound or not bound; or, as Professor Cowell renders it, ' O son of Gautama, is there any secret place in the world where thou canst set me unconnected, having fixed me there (as wood united with glue); or is there some other place where thou canst set me?' The speculations on the fate of the soul after death seem to have been peculiar to the royal families of India, while the Brahmans dwelt more on what may be called the shorter cut, a knowledge of Brahman as the true Self. To know, with them, was to be, and, after the dissolution of the body, they looked forward to immediate emancipation, without any further wanderings.
1 Worthy to know Brahman, or, as the commentator, who reads brahmargha, thinks, to be honoured like Brahman.
2 Both roads lead to the moon, and diverge afterwards.

to be born again K Verily, the moon is the door of the Svarga world (the heavenly world). Now, if a man objects to the moon (if one is not satisfied with life there) the moon sets him free2. But if a man does not object, then the moon sends him down as rain upon this earth. And according to his deeds and according to his knowledge he is born again here as a worm, or as an insect, or as a fish, or as a bird, or as a lion, or as a boar, or as a serpent 3, or as a tiger, or as a man, or as something else in different places 4. When he has thus returned to the earth, some one (a sage) asks : ' Who art thou ? ' And he should answer : ' From the wise moon, who orders the seasons5, when it is born consisting of fifteen parts, from the moon who is the home of our ancestors, the seed was brought. This seed, even me, they (the gods mentioned in the Pan/£agnividya6) gathered up in an active man, and through an active 1 I should like to read aparapakshe praganayati, instead of aparapakshewa, or aparapakshe na. The negative is out of the question, for prag-anayati, he sends into a new life, is exactly what the moon does to those who do not proceed on the Devapatha to the Brahmaloka. Therefore if the reading aparapakshewa must be retained, it should be rendered by 'the moon with the dark half sends them into a new life.'

2 This is supposed to be the hidden place, or rather the way to it, when the departed leave the moon, and pass on to lightning and to the world of Brahman. This is in fact the Devayana, as opposed to the Titriyana, described in the AV/andogya-upanishad.
3 Parajva, dandajukavijesha^. There is no authority for translating it by dog ; cf. Indische Studien I, 396.
4 This might even include naraka or hell.

B If rztava^ is here the genitive of n'tu, its meaning would be the ordainer of the seasons; cf. Hibbert Lectures, p. 247. ViX'akshawa is applied to the moon again, II, 9, and the throne of Brahman also is called vi/^aksha«a, I, 3.

I ADHYAYA, 3. 275 man they brought me to a mother. Then I, growing up to be born, a being living by months, whether twelve or thirteen, was together with my father, who also lived by (years of) twelve or thirteen months, that I might either know it (the true Brahman) or not know it. Therefore, O ye seasons 1, grant that I may attain immortality (knowledge of Brahman). By this my true saying, by this my toil (beginning with the dwelling in the moon and ending with my birth on earth) I am (like) a season, and the child of the seasons.' ' Who art thou ? ' the sage asks again. ' I am thou,' he replies. Then he sets him free 2 (to proceed onward).
zh: 16. 「已足夠矣，讓他認識此為真。
17. 「不知此者，既不應傳授他人，亦不應與不知者共餐，更不應與之共樂。」
隨後是一些關於通常如何誦讀《吠陀》的進一步規則：
18. 「當舊水乾涸時（約在波沙月之後，即公曆一月至二月2），即樹根周圍積水乾涸時，他不應誦讀；在早晨或午後亦不應誦讀，此時影子重合（他應在日出時開始誦讀，待影子分開之際；在日落前影子再次重合之時結束）。烏雲升起時，他亦不應誦讀；當發生不時之雨時（在史瓦瓦那月與婆陀羅跋提月之後，即八月與九月4），他應停止吠陀誦讀三個晚間。此時他不應講述故事，即使在夜間亦不應如此，也不應自誇其知識。」
19.「這（如此學習和研究的吠陀）乃是那偉大存在者之名；凡知曉那偉大存在者之名者，彼乃梵，誠然，彼乃梵。」
1 《阿帕斯坦巴經》，比勒譯，第92頁（I, 2, 30, 4）。
2 阿帕斯坦巴經（Apastamba-sutras），比勒譯，第33頁（I, 3, 9, 2）。
3 Apastamba-sutras，比勒爾譯，第 44 頁（I, 3, n, 31）。
4 Apastamba-sutras，比勒（Biihler）譯，第33頁（I, 3, 9, 1）。
考希塔基梵天奧義書（Kaushitaki-Brahmana-Upanishad）
第一阿地雅耶（First Adhyaya）
艾特剌‧剛迦亞尼（Aitra Gangyayani）欲執行祭祀，乃選擇阿魯姆（烏達拉卡 Uddalaka）為其祭司長。然阿魯姆遣其子濕維塔凱圖（Svetaketu）前往，曰：「為其執行祭祀。」濕維塔凱圖既至，艾特剌問之曰：「高多瑪之子（Son of Gautama），世界上是否有一隱密之處，汝得以將吾置於其中？抑或相反，汝將置吾於其（該隱密之處）所通往之世界中5？」

難以確定伊提特剌（iTitra）的名字究竟是迦刚雅尼（Gahgyayani）還是迦刚耶吒（Gargyaya/zi）。韋伯教授起初採納迦刚耶吒（《印度研究》第一卷，第395頁），其後改採迦刚雅尼（同上，第二卷，第395頁）。考厄爾教授採用迦刚雅尼，但他告訴我們泰盧固文手稿全文作迦刚耶吒，而其他手稿B、C則間或亦然。注釋家將迦刚雅尼解釋為迦刚雅之後裔（yuvapatyam）。我承認對迦刚耶吒有所偏好，因為迦刚與迦刚雅在古代吠陀文獻中都是罕見的名字，但我也承認，正因如此，迦刚雅尼轉變為迦刚耶目的解釋，也許比迦刚耶吒轉變為迦刚雅尼更為可理解。
2 參見《歡喜奧義書》第五卷第二部分第二節；《梵書》第六卷第二部分第一節。
3 參考《歌者奧義書》(Kh. Up.) V, 3；VI, 1。4 《梵書》(B/-zh. Ar.) VI, 2, 4。
艾特里（A'itra）向濕瓦多奎圖（Śvetaketu）所提出的問題極其晦澀，從一開始其措辭本身就可能被有意設為晦澀。艾特里想要提問的內容，我們可以從奧義書的其他段落推斷出來，在那裡我們看到另一位王仙普拉瓦哈那‧賈伊瓦利（Pravahana Gaivali，《歌者奧義書》第五篇第三章；《白驢奧義書》第六篇第二章）啟蒙濕瓦多奎圖關於來世的事。那個來世是經由兩條路可以到達的；
272 考希他基奧義書（KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD） 他回答說：「我不知道。但是，讓我去請教老師。」於是他走近自己的父親，問道：「阿特里（A'itra）這樣問我；我應該如何回答？」

其一為提婆路（Devapatha），通往梵天之世（有條件者），其外尚有一階段，乃無條件梵天之知識與同一性；其二則通往祖先之世，自此處消盡善業之果報後，復歸於新一輪的世俗存在之中。第三條路乃為蟲蚓、昆蟲及爬行生物等活生而後死亡之生物，但其影響微乎其微。 現在甚為明白，國王伊帝剌（iTitra）所具有而濕濕多（Svetaketu）所不具有之知識，乃是死後兩條路之知識，有時稱為右路與左路，或南路與北路。此兩條路在《喬答多奧義書》（Chandogya-upanishad）與《白駿梵經》（Brihadaranyaka）中詳細記述，雖有若干異同，然其總體目的則相同。北路或左路，亦稱提婆之路，自光明與白晝而行，經月球之明半；南路或右路，亦稱祖先之路，自煙塵與黑夜而行，經月球之暗半。兩路因此在月球相會，其後乃相分。北路於太陽向北移行之六月間，經日、（月）、閃電而至梵天之世，而南路則於太陽向南移行之六月間，至祖先之世、乙太及月球。然而兩路之大差異在於，前路之旅者不再返回地上而獲得新生，乃至終達無條件梵天之真知識，而經後路至祖先之世及月球者，則復返地上而生死輪迴不息。

阿魯尼因此對施瑞陀庫問及的問題，只能涉及這兩條道路，儘管原文極為訛誤，甚至在註釋成書之時顯然已然如此，我們必須試圖根據阿魯尼在後文所闡述的教說來復原之。我主張讀作：Gautamasya putra, asti saśravn'taw loke yasmin ma dhasyasy anyatamo vadhva tasya (or yasya) ma loke dhasyasi，「世間中是否有隱祕之處，你（藉由祭祀與教導）能夠在彼處……」[第 2 . 27 阿耀]。 阿魯尼說：「我亦不知此事。唯有在阿特拉自身的住處學得吠陀之正確部分後，我們方能獲得他人所給予之物（知識）。來吧，我們兩人一同前往。」
說完這番話後，他手持柴薪（如同學生般），走近題多羅‧剛迦亞尼（TTitra Gangyayani），說道：「我可以靠近你嗎？」他回答：「高塔馬啊，你配得上梵天，因為你未被驕傲所迷。來此處吧，我將使你明白一切。」

2. 而伊蒂特羅（Ititra）說：凡離開此世界（或此身體）者，皆往月球去。2. 在前者，即（明亮的）半月，月球以其靈魂自樂；在另一者，即（黑暗的）半月，月球將他們送往——請問我，或者反之亦然，你會將我置於它所通往的世界中嗎？即使如此，本文也絕非令人滿意，但仍優於評論家所採用的 anyam aho vadhva，而被他解釋為：在那個世界中是否存在一個隱密之地，你將在其中以他人身分置我，即作為不同於整個世界者，或與整個世界相同者？若作為不同者，則你是否已束縛我（vadhva = baddhva）並使我成為一個不同的人？我們可以讀作 anyataro 以代替 anyatamo vadhva。評論家將問題總結為涉及一隱密或非隱密之地，其中伊蒂特羅應被置為另一個人或非另一個人，作為被束縛或非被束縛者；或如考威爾教授所譯者，「高塔瑪之子，世界中是否有任何祕密之所，汝能將我置於其中而不相連繫，將我固定於彼（如木為膠所聯）；或是否有其他某地，汝能將我置於其中？」論及死後靈魂之命運的思辨似乎為印度皇族所特有，而婆羅門則更關注所謂的捷徑，即作為真實自我的梵天（Brahman）之知識。對他們而言，知即為有，身體消解後，他們期待著直接的解脫，而無任何進一步的流轉。
值得認識梵天（Brahman），或如讀作 brahmargha 的註釋者所認為的，應當像尊崇梵天一樣受到尊敬。
兩條道路都通往月亮，之後才分歧開來。

再生 K 真實言之，月亮乃是天界（Svarga world）之門。今若人反對月亮（若人對彼處之生命不滿），則月亮使其獲得解脫２。惟若人不反對，則月亮將其作為雨水送下至此地球。且依其業行與其知識，彼乃於此處再生為蟲，或為昆蟲，或為魚，或為鳥，或為獅，或為豬，或為蛇３，或為虎，或為人，或為其他物類於不同之處４。當彼如此返回地球時，有人（一位賢者）問曰：「汝是誰？」彼應當答曰：「自智慧之月——彼命令四時者——當其誕生而具十五部分時，自月亮——我等祖先之家——此種子被帶來。此種子，即我也，彼等（上述《泛食識論》(Pañcagnividya)６中所言及之諸神）於一位活躍之人身上集聚之，且透過一位活躍¹ --- ¹ 我傾向於讀作 aparapakshe praganayati，而非 aparapakshewa 或 aparapakshe na。否定詞無疑問，因為 prag-anayati（彼將之送入新生命）正是月亮對不走提婆道路（Devapatha）而往梵天界（Brahmaloka）者所為。是故若必須保留 aparapakshewa 之讀法，應譯為「月亮以其暗半將彼等送入新生命」。
這據稱是隱祕之處，或毋寧說是往彼處的途徑，亡者離開月亮，進而經過閃電，抵達梵的世界。這實際上就是《阿特瓦亞奧義書》（Aitareya-upanishad）中所述的「提婆之道」（Devayana），與「提得利耶那之道」（Pitriyana）相對立。
3 Parajva, dandajukavijesha^。將其翻譯為狗（dog）並無權威依據；參照《印度研究》第一卷，第 396 頁。
這甚至可能包括那羅迦（naraka）或地獄。
B 若 Rtava 在此為 rtu 的屬格形式，其意義為「季節的制定者」；參見《希伯特講座》第 247 頁。Viśākshava 再次被用於月亮，《梨俱吠陀》II, 9，而梵天的王座也被稱為 viśākshava，I, 3。
6 《歌者奧義書》第五卷，第四至八章。

I ADHYAYA, 3. 275 人們把我帶到了一位母親那裡。之後，我長大成長要被生下來，一個按月份生活的生命體，無論是十二個月還是十三個月，我與我的父親在一起，他也生活在（十二或十三個月的）月份中，為的是我或許能認識它（真實的梵）或不認識它。因此，啊眾位季節啊，願我得以達到不朽（對梵的認識）。藉著我真實的言語，藉著我的勞作（開始於在月亮中的居住，終止於我在地上的誕生），我（如同）一個季節，並且是眾季節的孩子。' 你是誰？' 智者再次問道。' 我是你，' 他回答。隨後他釋放了他²（讓他繼續前進）。
